title: PaRDeS: The Four Levels of Torah Interpretation
description: Explaining pshat, remez, drash, and sod — and how the same verse can contain multiple layers of meaning.
author: Alice (Torah Light Writer)
sources: Scholem, Gershom - Major Trends in Jewish Mysticism (1946)
Fishbane, Michael - The Garments of Torah: Essays in Biblical Hermeneutics (1989)
Idel, Moshe - Absorbing Perfections: Kabbalah and Interpretation (2002)
header_image_description: A mystical Chinese garden with four distinct levels connected by stone pathways. The bottom level shows scholars studying texts at wooden tables (representing Peshat), the second level features teachers and students in animated discussion under pavilions (Remez), the third level displays philosophers contemplating beneath ancient trees (Drash), and the highest level shows a solitary figure in meditation surrounded by swirling clouds and ethereal light (Sod). Each level is painted in progressively more ethereal tones, from earthly browns and greens at the bottom to luminous gold and white at the top. Traditional Chinese architectural elements blend with Middle Eastern scrolls and Hebrew letters floating in the air. Style: traditional Chinese landscape painting (shan shui) with mystical elements, fine brushwork, and spiritual depth.
